questions.to_find: The output printed by the C program involving bitwise operations on characters

understand.plain: The program assigns ASCII character values to 'a' and 'b', then performs bitwise AND, OR, and XOR operations on them, adds or subtracts other character constants, and prints the resulting characters. We need to trace the arithmetic using ASCII values to find what characters c, d, and e become.

keywords.explain: Operates on each bit of two integers; the result bit is 1 only if both input bits are 1.
Operates on each bit; the result bit is 1 if at least one input bit is 1.
Operates on each bit; the result bit is 1 if the input bits differ.
Each character maps to an integer. In C, char arithmetic uses these integer values.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='520' height='220'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='500' height='200' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='20' y='38' class='lbl ink'>a = 'P' = 80 = 01010000</text><text x='20' y='62' class='lbl ink'>b = 'x' = 120 = 01111000</text><rect x='10' y='72' width='500' height='2' class='bg' style='fill:#B4B2A9'/><text x='20' y='100' class='sub ink'>a & b = 01010000 & 01111000 = 01010000 = 80</text><text x='20' y='120' class='sub ink'>c = 80 + '*'(42) = 122 = 'z'</text><text x='20' y='148' class='sub ink'>a | b = 01010000 | 01111000 = 01111000 = 120</text><text x='20' y='168' class='sub ink'>d = 120 - '-'(45) = 75 = 'K'</text><text x='20' y='196' class='sub ink'>a ^ b = 01010000 ^ 01111000 = 00101000 = 40;  e = 40 + '+'(43) = 83 = 'S'</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Bitwise operations table showing binary representations of 'P'(80) and 'x'(120), computing AND=80, OR=120, XOR=40, then adding/subtracting ASCII offsets to get characters z, K, S

given.aim: Determine the three characters printed after applying bitwise operations and arithmetic on ASCII values of 'P' and 'x'

terms.example: P is the 16th uppercase letter; ASCII = 64 + 16 = 80
terms.connects: Used as left operand in all three bitwise operations

terms.example: x is the 24th lowercase letter; ASCII = 96 + 24 = 120
terms.connects: Used as right operand in all three bitwise operations

terms.meaning: ASCII values of the operator characters used as numeric offsets
terms.example: '*' in C is an integer 42 when used in arithmetic
terms.connects: Added or subtracted to the bitwise results to yield the final character codes

given.plan: Convert 'P' and 'x' to their 8-bit ASCII values. Compute bitwise AND, OR, and XOR. Add or subtract the ASCII values of the given constants. Convert the resulting integers back to characters using the ASCII table.

steps.title: ASCII values and binary representations
steps.apply: P is the 16th letter of the alphabet; uppercase letters start at 65, so P = 65 + 15 = 80. x is the 24th lowercase letter; lowercase start at 97, so x = 97 + 23 = 120. In binary: 80 = 01010000, 120 = 01111000.
steps.note: The two values share bits in positions 4, 5, 6 (0-indexed from right), making the bitwise results easy to verify.

steps.title: Compute c = (a & b) + '*'
steps.apply: AND keeps only bits set in BOTH: 01010000 & 01111000 = 01010000 = 80. Adding '*' (ASCII 42): 80 + 42 = 122. ASCII 122 = 'z'.
steps.note: The AND result equals a (80) here because every set bit of 'P' is also set in 'x'.

steps.title: Compute d = (a | b) - '-'
steps.apply: OR keeps bits set in EITHER: 01010000 | 01111000 = 01111000 = 120. Subtracting '-' (ASCII 45): 120 - 45 = 75. ASCII 75 = 'K'.
steps.note: The OR result equals b (120) because all bits of a are already present in b.

steps.title: Compute e = (a ^ b) + '+'
steps.note: XOR gives bits present in one but not both; positions 3 and 5 differ between 80 and 120.

steps.title: Final output
steps.note: Each value is printed as a character (%c format specifier), not as a number.

solution.result: The program prints: z K S (Option A)

questions.formula_note: Bitwise AND, OR, XOR on ASCII integer values of characters, combined with character-constant arithmetic in C.

questions.to_find: The integer value printed by the program using a function with a static local variable

understand.plain: The program defines a function 'funcp' that has a static integer variable 'x' initialized to 1. A static local variable retains its value between function calls unlike a normal local variable. The function increments x and returns it. The main function calls funcp twice and prints the sum of the two return values.

keywords.explain: A local variable declared with 'static' is initialized only once and retains its value across multiple calls to the function.
keywords.example: static int count = 0; count++; — count persists between calls; after 3 calls it is 3.

keywords.explain: The value the function sends back to the caller via the return statement.
keywords.example: int f(){ return 42; } — calling f() gives 42.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='420' height='180'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='400' height='160' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='20' y='40' class='lbl ink'>static x initialized to 1 (once only)</text><rect x='20' y='55' width='170' height='50' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='30' y='75' class='sub ink'>1st call: x++ -> x=2</text><text x='30' y='95' class='sub ink'>returns 2  (x=funcp())</text><rect x='210' y='55' width='185' height='50' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='220' y='75' class='sub ink'>2nd call: x++ -> x=3</text><text x='220' y='95' class='sub ink'>returns 3  (y=funcp())</text><text x='20' y='140' class='lbl ink'>printf: x + y = 2 + 3 = 5</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two-call trace diagram: static variable starts at 1, first call increments to 2 (returned as x=2), second call increments to 3 (returned as y=3), sum printed is 5

given.aim: Find the printed value of x+y where x and y are return values of two successive calls to funcp(), which uses a static local variable

terms.meaning: Static local variable initialized to 1 exactly once at program start; retains its value between calls
terms.example: First call sees x=1, increments to 2; second call sees x=2, increments to 3
terms.connects: This persistence is why the two calls return different values (2 and 3)

terms.meaning: Increments x by 1, then the incremented value is returned
terms.example: If x=1, after x++ x=2; return x returns 2
terms.connects: Each call increases the static variable, so successive calls return successive integers

terms.meaning: Local variables in main that store the return values of the two funcp calls
terms.example: x = funcp() gives 2; y = funcp() gives 3
terms.connects: Their sum is what gets printed

given.plan: Trace the static variable through each function call. First call: static x starts at 1, x++ makes it 2, returns 2. Second call: static x is now 2, x++ makes it 3, returns 3. Sum = 2 + 3 = 5.

steps.title: Initialization of static variable
steps.apply: The static variable x inside funcp is initialized to 1 exactly once when the program starts. It is NOT reinitialized on subsequent calls.
steps.note: This is the key property of static local variables — unlike automatic (local) variables which get a fresh copy each call.

steps.title: First call: x = funcp()
steps.apply: When funcp is called the first time, the static x is 1. x++ increments it to 2. The function returns x which is now 2. So the variable x in main receives 2.
steps.note: x++ is a pre-increment on the statement level here: the statement x++; increments x to 2, and then return x; returns the new value 2.

steps.title: Second call: y = funcp()
steps.apply: Static x retains value 2 from the first call. x++ increments it to 3. The function returns 3. The variable y in main receives 3.
steps.note: The static variable is shared across all calls; it remembers 2 from the previous call.

steps.title: Compute and print x + y
steps.apply: x=2 (from 1st call), y=3 (from 2nd call). x + y = 5. The program prints 5.
steps.note: The local variable 'x' in main shadows the static 'x' in funcp; they are completely separate variables despite having the same name.

questions.formula_note: Static local variable lifetime and persistence across function calls in C.
